deals_cleared.merger_name: RDO Australia – freehold land and buildings of three commercial truck dealership sites in West Wodonga VIC

acquirers.name: RDO AUSTRALIA PROPERTY HOLDINGS NO 2 PTY LTD

deals_cleared.merger_description: CD & JB Investments Pty Ltd (the **Vendor**) owns, and supplies commercial leasing services in respect of, three commercial truck dealership sites in West Wodonga, Victoria (collectively, the **Target Premises**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
RDO Australia Property Holdings No 2 Pty Ltd (RDO Australia)
applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
acquisition of 100% of the freehold land interest and buildings of
three commercial truck dealership sites located in West Wodonga,
Victoria (the Target Premises), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
6 June 2026

The acquirer, RDO Australia, owns and leases three commercial truck
dealership sites in Wagga Wagga, Dubbo and Orange (NSW).
The Target Premises are currently owned by CD & JB Investments
Pty Ltd (the Vendor).
The Target Premises are currently leased by the Vendor to Inland
Truck Centres Pty Ltd, a commercial truck dealership business and
wholly owned subsidiary of RDO Australia.
In 2025, Inland Truck Centres Pty Ltd acquired 100% of the business
assets (excluding land) of the commercial truck dealership which
operated from the Target Premises, which was a wholly owned
subsidiary of the Vendor.
After the Acquisition, RDO Australia will own the Target Premises
and Inland Truck Centres Pty Ltd will continue to operate its
commercial truck dealership business from the Target Premises.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application, and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap between the parties (and their
connected entities) in the supply of commercial leasing
services or commercial trucks in Wodonga or the
surrounding areas
b. RDO Australia, via its subsidiary Inland Trucks, is currently
leasing the land and intends to continue using the land for
the same purposes, but as an owner
c. there are alternative truck dealerships in Wodonga and the
surrounding areas.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:
TWG Hunter Dealerships Pty Ltd’s (TWG Hunter Dealerships)
proposed acquisition of 95% of the share capital of Sharton Motors
Pty Ltd (Sharton Motors) (together, the Parties) and the land on
which the motor dealership businesses owned by Sharton Motors
are operated (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, TWG Hunter Dealerships is a special purpose vehicle
associated with the Tony White Group (TWG). TWG operates a
network of vehicle dealerships supplying new and used vehicles, as
well as car servicing and repairs and OEM spare parts, across New
South Wales (NSW), Queensland, Victoria and Tasmania.
In the Newcastle and Hunter Valley region of NSW, TWG operates:
• Cardiff Motor Group (comprised of Cardiff Ford, Nissan,
Hyundai, Chery, and Volkswagen)
• Highways Group in Rutherford (comprised of Highway Ford
and Hyundai)
• Kloster Motor Group (dealing Ford, Mitsubishi, Hyundai,
Volkswagen, Nissan, Suzuki, Chery and other brands).
The target, Sharton Motors operates four dealerships in the
Newcastle and Hunter Valley region:
• Maitland Toyota
• Hunter GWM
• Cardiff GWM
• Port Stephens Toyota.
Sharton Motors’ dealerships supply new Toyota and GWM / Haval
motor vehicles, used motor vehicles, car servicing and repairs, and
OEM spare parts.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The Parties overlap in the Newcastle and Hunter Valley region of
NSW in the supply of:
• new cars
• used cars
• car servicing and repairs
• OEM spare parts.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, further information and documents provided by the
Parties, market inquiry feedback, and publicly available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
Regarding the supply of new cars:
• The market share aggregation resulting from the Acquisition is
estimated to be low.
• Post-Acquisition, the merged firm would be expected to face
competition from multiple alternative suppliers of new cars, both
on an intra-brand and inter-brand basis.
Regarding the supply of used cars:
• The market share aggregation resulting from the Acquisition is
estimated to be low.
• Post-Acquisition, the merged firm would be expected to face
competition from multiple alternative suppliers of used cars.
Regarding the supply of car servicing and repairs and OEM spare
parts:
• Given the Parties do not overlap on an intra-brand basis, the
Parties are not likely to be close competitors in the supply of car
servicing and repairs, and OEM spare parts (which dealerships
generally only provide for the OEM brands they supply).

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: OpenAI Group PBC, an entity controlled by the OpenAI Foundation (**OpenAI**), or another entity controlled by OpenAI, proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Tomoro AI Ltd (**Tomoro**).

events.title: OpenAI-Tomoro - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
The proposed acquisition of 100% of the share capital in Tomoro AI
Ltd (Tomoro) by OpenAI Group PBC, an entity controlled by the
OpenAI Foundation (OpenAI) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, OpenAI, develops artificial intelligence (AI) foundation
models, including its GPT family of models, as well as foundation
model-based services, including ChatGPT.
The target, Tomoro, is an AI delivery consultancy that helps
businesses implement AI solutions built around commercially
available generative AI technology (with a focus on OpenAI's
models). Tomoro helps businesses to improve the use of AI within
the business and provides assistance with building in-house
solutions (based on existing commercial foundation models) for
specific business needs.

There is a non-horizontal relationship between OpenAI and Tomoro
(together, the Parties), as Tomoro provides its AI consultancy and
implementation services almost exclusively in respect of OpenAI’s
foundation models and application program interfaces (APIs).
There is limited horizontal overlap between the Parties in the supply
of enterprise-focused AI consulting and implementation services.
OpenAI deploys engineers to a limited customer base, typically on a
long-term arrangement to provide highly technical support. Tomoro
offers its consultancy services to a broad range of business
customers, including for shorter term deployments and
consultations, and provides more holistic and strategic advice.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and publicly available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings:
• There is limited horizontal overlap between the AI consulting and
implementation services offered by OpenAI and Tomoro, given
differences in their customer bases and the different types of
services provided and given Tomoro has a very limited customer
base in Australia.
• Post-Acquisition, the merged entity would be unlikely to have the
ability and incentive to foreclose rival downstream AI consulting
and implementation service providers’ access to AI foundation
models, given the availability of multiple alternative suppliers of
AI foundation models, including Anthropic, Google, Microsoft,
Meta, xAI, MistralAI and others.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

determination_table_content.details:
News Corp Australia Pty Ltd (News Corp)’s proposed acquisition
(through its subsidiary, News Pty Ltd) of a 20% interest in National
Distribution Services Pty Ltd (NDS) and a call option to acquire the
remaining shares in NDS in limited circumstances (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, News Corp, is an Australian media company with
interests across digital media, magazines, newspapers, audio
production, marketing and publishing. It is indirectly wholly owned by
News Corporation, which is headquartered in the United States and
listed on the Nasdaq and ASX.
Through its Australian subsidiaries, News Corp currently contracts
with a range of suppliers, including NDS, to distribute its print media
publications across Australia to retailers and home delivery
subscribers. News Corp also arranges for its suppliers to distribute
certain third-party publishers’ print media on a shared carriage
basis.
The target, NDS, is an Australian distribution and logistics company
which supplies services to a range of publishers in Australia,
including News Corp. NDS operates a distribution network covering
Victoria, New South Wales, South Australia, Tasmania, Queensland
and Darwin.
The reasons for the ACCC’s determination are set out in section 2.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.
For the reasons given below, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect.
Relationship between the parties

Based on the material before it, the ACCC finds that News Corp and NDS (together, the Parties) primarily interact through a vertical relationship, given that NDS supplies News Corp with distribution services for its print media. While they may in theory presently compete for some publisher customers (via News Corp’s shared carriage arrangements), the ACCC has formed the view based on information provided by the Parties that they are unlikely to do so in the future with or without the Acquisition.

In addition to acquiring a 20% interest in NDS through the proposed Acquisition, News Corp has announced that it intends to partner with NDS to create a new business, Reach Plus, that will streamline large-scale deliveries of its publications to regional areas of Australia. The ACCC understands that any spare capacity in Reach Plus’s newspaper trucks will be sold to other businesses that wish to distribute their printed products in country areas.1
Industry background

The distribution of print media such as newspapers and magazines generally encompasses linehaul distribution, (i.e. delivering the print media from various printing sites to ‘hubs’ for subsequent distribution) and secondary distribution, (whereby the print media is delivered from these hubs or directly from printing sites to retailers or end customers).

There has been a general trend of declining print media readership in Australia. This has prompted publishers to identify efficiencies to address reduced demand and rising costs associated with producing and distributing print publications. Many of these publishers have sought to reduce their distribution costs in various ways, such as by outsourcing distribution to specialist distribution service providers that lower costs by combining delivery runs (as opposed to the traditional model where secondary distribution was often performed by newsagents).

Similarly, in response to commercial and economic challenges facing the publishing industry, many traditional suppliers of distribution services for print media (such as newsagents) have exited the industry in recent years.
Competition assessment

The ACCC has considered the effects of the Acquisition by comparing the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition proceeds against the continuation of the current state of competition (being the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition does not proceed).
Relevant areas of competition
The ACCC considered whether the Acquisition is likely to substantially lessen competition by providing News Corp with:

items: the ability and incentive to successfully foreclose rival print media publishers by limiting their access to NDS’s distribution services and/or
the ability and incentive to foreclose NDS’s print media distribution rivals by limiting their access to sufficient distribution volumes of print media.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: The ACCC’s assessment regarding each of these points is discussed further below. 1 James Madden, ‘News looks to share load in regional communities’, The Australian, 4 May 2026.

In addition, the ACCC initially considered other possible theories of harm, including potential unilateral horizontal effects in the supply of distribution services to print media publishers. This theory was considered and dismissed as it was not supported by the material before the ACCC.
In conducting its analysis, the ACCC had regard to information and documents provided by the Parties and information provided by third parties.
For the purposes of its assessment, the ACCC did not consider it necessary to form a concluded view on the precise geographic scope of the relevant markets.
Vertical effects – foreclosure of rival print media publishers by limiting their access to or raising the price of distribution services

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in the supply of print media publication services by enabling News Corp to foreclose its rival print media publishers through limiting their access to or increasing the price of distribution services. This is because the ACCC’s assessment is that it is unlikely News Corp would have the incentive to fully or partially foreclose rival print media publishers in this way.
Based on market feedback and information provided by the Parties, the ACCC makes the following findings:

items: News Corp may have the ability to foreclose rival print media publishers in certain metropolitan and regional areas through limiting rivals’ access to or increasing the price of NDS’s distribution services in those areas. o In certain metropolitan and regional areas of Australia, where NDS has extensive scale and scope in the supply of print newspaper distribution services, rival distribution service providers appear to offer weak competitive alternatives. o For some print media publishers, there are few or no genuine alternative suppliers to NDS for some or all of their distribution needs, and self-supply of distribution services is not economically feasible. o News Corp, via its influence over NDS, appears likely to have the ability to limit its rivals’ access to NDS’ distribution services through raising NDS’ prices or refusing to supply distribution services to rivals.

However, News Corp would be unlikely to have the incentive to fully or partially foreclose rival print media publishers in this way, because: o The profitability of such a foreclosure strategy would require a material number of consumers to switch away from rival print publications to News Corp’s own print media rather than simply switching from the rival’s print media to the rival’s digital media. o Scale and scope efficiencies, which are very important to the ongoing viability of the NDS business, would only likely be achieved by continuing to supply distribution services to rival publishers, and to the extent that any foreclosure reduced NDS’s volumes below efficient levels it would likely raise NewsCorp’s own costs as well as those of its rival publishers.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: The ACCC also notes that News Corp currently provides printing services to rival new media publishers across Australia. The ACCC considers it unlikely that News Corp would have the incentive to foreclose rival news media publishers from access to distribution services given it apparently does not have the incentive to foreclose rival news media publishers’ access to printing services, despite an absence of printing options available to rival news media publishers. This is because the margins that would be lost to News Corp, via NDS, from foreclosing rivals’ access to news media print distribution post-Acquisition would necessarily involve the loss of margins to News Corp from printing rivals’ publications as well as the margins lost from distributing rivals’ publications. The combined effect of these lost margins on News Corp must be higher than the margins which would have been lost had News Corp adopted a strategy of foreclosing rivals’ access to printing services. Accordingly, taking into account the continued provision of printing services to News Corp’s rival news media publishers, it appears unlikely News Corp would have the incentive, via NDS, to foreclose rival news media publishers’ access to distribution services post- Acquisition.
Vertical effects – foreclosure of rival distribution service suppliers by limiting their access to sufficient distribution volumes of print media

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in the supply of print media distribution services by limiting NDS’s rivals’ access to sufficient distribution volumes of print media.

This is because, based on information and documents provided by the Parties, the ACCC finds, that under both a future with and a future without the Acquisition, it is likely that News Corp would be incentivised to improve the efficiency of its print media distribution model over time by consolidating its demand to a smaller number of distribution contractors than it currently uses.

While the ACCC recognises that NDS’s rival distribution service providers may face reduced demand over time, the ACCC considers that any reduction in competition that results from this reduced demand is not due to the Acquisition.

events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Paramount Skydance Corporation (Paramount) and Warner Bros.
Discovery, Inc. (Warner Brothers) signed an agreement (the Merger
Agreement). Under the terms of the Merger Agreement, Paramount
will pay USD $31.00 per share in cash for all outstanding shares of
Warner Brothers (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Paramount, is a global media, streaming and
entertainment company that supplies content and experiences,
including films, television series, and animated content.
Paramount’s portfolio of brands and production assets includes
Paramount Pictures, Paramount Television, Paramount+, CBS, CBS
News, CBS Sports, Nickelodeon, MTV, BET, Comedy Central,
Showtime, Pluto TV and Skydance Media LLC animation,
interactive/games and sports divisions.
In Australia, Paramount operates the free-to-air (FTA) television
network, Network 10. Network 10 is one of five Australian FTA TV
networks, and operates four television channels (Channel 10, 10
Drama, 10 Comedy and Nickelodeon) and a free online streaming
service (‘10’). Paramount also operates Paramount+, a paid digital
subscription video on-demand (SVOD) and live streaming service
available to Australian consumers.
Paramount also licenses audiovisual content to rival TV channels
and digital services in Australia.
Paramount distributes films to cinemas in Australia through its local
theatrical distribution company.
The target, Warner Brothers, is a global media and entertainment
company that creates and distributes a portfolio of content and
products across television, film, streaming and interactive gaming.
Warner Brothers’ brands and production assets include Discovery
Channel, CNN, DC Studios, New Line Cinema, TNT Sports, Eurosport,
HBO Max, HBO, Warner Bros. Motion Picture Group, Warner Bros.
Television Group, and Warner Bros. Games.
Warner Brothers operates several pay TV services in Australia
(Discovery, Investigation Discovery, Animal Planet, TLC, Discovery
Turbo, Travel Channel and HGTV) and the SVOD service HBO Max.
In Australia, Warner Brothers distributes films to cinemas via a third
party, Universal Pictures, and has local subsidiaries active in film and
television production, including Big Pond Productions Pty Ltd and
Warner Bros. International Television Production Australia.
The reasons for the ACCC’s determination are set out in section 2.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.
For the reasons given below, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect.
Industry background
Wholesale production, licensing and distribution of theatrical and other AV content

Paramount and Warner Brothers (together, the Parties) are both active in the production, licensing and distribution of both theatrical audio visual (AV) content (i.e. films for theatrical release in cinemas), and other AV content focused on television and home entertainment.

items.text: Production of AV content involves financing, script development, casting, shooting and post-production, either by one studio or through a collaboration between studios.
Licensing of AV content refers to producers licensing the distribution rights to their content to downstream companies (e.g. wholesale distributors, cinemas, streaming services, etc.).

For both theatrical and other AV content, distribution is carried out through either third-party distributors who specialise in the distribution of content, or self- distribution by producers directly to retail providers of AV content.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: These three activities are sometimes referred to as wholesale activities in that they are supplied to downstream businesses (e.g., streaming services, cinemas) rather than consumers. Retail provision of theatrical and other AV content to consumers
The display of AV content to end consumers, which is sometimes referred to as retail supply or provision, can be categorised as follows.

Theatrical content is displayed to end consumers by cinemas. There are 466 cinemas in Australia that exhibit theatrical content for consumption by customers.1 The Parties, as well as Walt Disney, Universal, Sony, Roadshow Films, Madman Entertainment, Icon and other studios supply theatrical content that is exhibited in cinemas. 1 Screen Australia, Cinema Industry Trends: Screens and Theatres.
Non-theatrical AV content is displayed to end consumers via several channels
(together, non-theatrical retail AV services), including:

items.text: Linear TV channels (either free-to-air (FTA) TV or pay TV). There are a number of FTA networks in Australia that acquire AV content produced by the Parties and other producers to display to consumers. Seven West Media, Nine Entertainment and ABC are the three largest FTA networks in Australia by market share. Channel Ten is another FTA network, which is owned by a subsidiary of Paramount.

Subscription video on demand (SVOD) services. There are many SVOD services in Australia, including those provided by the Parties (Paramount+ and HBO Max), as well as Netflix, Stan, Disney, Binge, Apple TV, Amazon Prime, BritBox, Hayu, Kayo and Crunchyroll. The providers of these services may display their own content and/or license content from third parties (including from the Parties). A 2025 survey from Australian Communications and Media Authority found that SVOD services are the most popular way for Australians to watch video content, with 68% of Australian adults using SVOD services.2

Other channels, including ad-supported video on demand (AVOD) services, free ad-supported streaming TV (FAST) services, and the sale or rental of physical media (e.g. DVDs) and digital media (e.g. transactional video on demand (TVOD), pay-per-view).

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Many providers of non-theatrical retail AV services operate more than one of the above services; for example, providers of linear television also offer streaming services (either AVOD, TVOD or SVOD), which can include ‘catch up’ of content previously aired on linear TV.
Australian consumers often multi-home across services to access a broader range of AV content, using an average of 4.2 different services to watch video content online in 2025.3
Overlap and relationship between the Parties

Based on information provided by the Parties and market participants, as well as other publicly available information, the ACCC considers there are both horizontal and vertical relationships between the Parties as follows:

items.text: Wholesale supply of films for theatrical release (horizontal and vertical): The ACCC finds that the Parties’ activities overlap horizontally in the supply of films for theatrical release to Australian cinemas. The Parties are both major producers of films released theatrically worldwide. Paramount distributes its own films to cinemas in Australia via a local subsidiary, while Warner Brothers distributes films to cinemas in Australia via Universal Pictures. There is also a vertical relationship between the Parties as, following the initial release of films to cinemas, the Parties typically license these films to SVOD or other linear and on-demand retail AV services. 2 Australian Communications and Media Authority, Communications and media in Australia series: How we watch and listen to content – Executive summary and key findings (March 2026), p 2. 3 Australian Communications and Media Authority, Communications and media in Australia series: How we watch and listen to content – Executive summary and key findings (March 2026), p 6.

Wholesale supply of other AV content (horizontal and vertical): The Parties overlap horizontally and have a vertical relationship in the production and wholesale supply of non-theatrical AV content. The Parties both supply non- theatrical AV content to third-party TV networks, streaming platforms and other retail services, including each other’s. The Parties supply this content to third parties through the sale or licensing of rights to exhibit existing content, the production of content commissioned by a third-party network or platform, or co- financing a production where intellectual property rights to the finished content are split between the financers.

Retail provision of AV content to consumers (horizontal): The Parties overlap horizontally in the supply of non-theatrical retail AV services to consumers through their respective SVOD platforms, Paramount+ and HBO Max. Both platforms offer a range of content including TV series and films of various genres, and Paramount+ also offers live sport (primarily Australian A-League soccer). Paramount also operates in the retail provision of AV content to consumers through its ownership of the FTA television network, Network 10, which includes several terrestrial TV channels and a free online streaming service, 10.

Other overlaps: The Parties also overlap horizontally in the supply of advertising space, IP licensing rights for merchandise, and development and publishing of video games in Australia. However, the Parties have a limited presence in these markets.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Competition assessment

The ACCC has considered the effects of the Acquisition by comparing the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition proceeds against the continuation of the current state of competition (being the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition does not proceed).
Relevant areas of competition
The ACCC has considered whether the Acquisition is likely to substantially lessen competition in the production, licensing and wholesale supply of films for theatrical release in Australia.

The ACCC has also considered whether the Acquisition is likely to substantially lessen competition in the supply of non-theatrical retail AV services in Australia should the merged entity, post-Acquisition, seek to leverage its position in the supply of wholesale AV content to foreclose rivals.
In addition, the ACCC initially considered other possible theories of harm, including:

items.text: potential unilateral horizontal effects in the wholesale supply of non-theatrical AV content;
potential unilateral horizontal effects in the supply of SVOD services;
the potential for the merged entity to leverage its position in its downstream retail services (e.g. its SVOD services) to foreclose rival suppliers of wholesale non- theatrical AV content;
potential unilateral horizontal effects in the acquisition of AV production services in Australia; and
potential unilateral horizontal effects in the supply of advertising space.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: However, these theories were not considered in detail as they are not supported by the material before the ACCC.
Competition effects in the supply of films for theatrical release

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the wholesale supply of films for theatrical release in Australia. The ACCC finds that, while the Acquisition would remove competition between Paramount and Warner Brothers, the merged entity would continue to be constrained by other film studios post-Acquisition.

The ACCC has considered how each of the Parties compete in the supply of films for theatrical release in Australia. In reaching its decision, the ACCC has had regard to information provided by the Parties and market participants, as well as other publicly available information.
Based on the materials before it, the ACCC considers:

items.text: The materials do not support the view that Paramount and Warner Brothers are particularly close competitors or that they compete more closely with each other than with the other major film studios. The Parties, as well as major film studios like Disney, Sony and Universal Pictures, are key suppliers of films for theatrical release, routinely competing for exhibition by cinema operators and box office revenue.

Post-Acquisition, the merged entity would continue to face competition from alternative suppliers of films for theatrical release. The alternative suppliers include major studios Disney, Sony and Universal, which each received approximately 10%–25% share of annual Australian box office receipts between 2021 and 2025.4 The merged entity would also continue to face some competition from smaller or independent suppliers, such as Roadshow, Amazon MGM, StudioCanal, Madman, Icon, Rialto, Transmission, Pinnacle and other independent providers.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Market participants raised concerns that the Acquisition would result in the merged entity favouring its own vertically-integrated content distribution arms (e.g. its SVOD services) to foreclose competition by others, including third-party cinemas, by releasing more of its films directly to its own non-cinema content distribution arms without any prior theatrical release, or with only limited theatrical release. However, while vertically integrated film studios have incentives to provide films to their own SVOD services, the materials before the ACCC indicate that the Parties also have an incentive to first supply films to cinemas for theatrical release. Taking into account the above findings, the ACCC does not consider the current incentives, including the proposed timeframes for theatrical release, appear likely to substantially change for the merged entity post-Acquisition.

Some market participants also raised concerns that the Acquisition would result in a reduction in output of films produced by the merged entity (compared to the number 4 Screen Australia, Cinema Industry Trends Distributor Box Office Share. of films that the Parties currently produce), or a reduction in the quality or variety of films produced by the merged entity. However, based on materials before it, the ACCC considers that the merged entity would still be incentivised to produce and supply a similar number of films, and films of similar quality, to Australian cinemas given the competitive constraints it would continue to face from alternative suppliers of films for theatrical release, as noted above.
Foreclosure of rival non-theatrical retail AV services from accessing wholesale AV content

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in the supply of non-theatrical retail AV services, including SVOD services (e.g. Netflix, Binge) and linear TV services (e.g. Channel 9, Channel 7), by enabling the merged entity to foreclose rivals’ access to wholesale AV content.
In making its assessment, the ACCC has had regard to market shares, margins and subscriber data supplied by the Parties, as well as information supplied by market participants.

Based on the materials before it, the ACCC finds that the merged entity is unlikely to have a sufficiently strong position in the supply of wholesale AV content to enable it to successfully foreclose rivals’ access to AV content. In particular, while feedback from market participants indicates the Parties are important in the supply of AV content to non-theatrical retail AV services, there appear to be a range of alternative sources such as NBCUniversal, Sony, Lionsgate, Amazon MGM and local wholesale content suppliers (e.g. All3Media, Banijay, Fremantle, A&E). In addition, some TV networks and on-demand services have the option of self-supply by commissioning or producing their own content.

Therefore, the ACCC considers that Paramount and Warner Bros’ rival non-theatrical retail AV services suppliers are not sufficiently dependent on access to the Parties’ wholesale AV content. Accordingly, if the merged entity were to restrict rival suppliers’ access to its content, it would be unlikely to lead to a significant loss of rivals’ sales or reduction in their content quality. Therefore, the ACCC considers that there is unlikely to be a substantial lessening of competition arising from foreclosure of access to wholesale AV content.

deals_cleared.merger_description: AtkinsRéalis Group Inc. through its wholly owned subsidiary AtkinsRéalis Australia Holdings Pty Ltd (**AtkinsRéalis**) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in WGA Group Holdings Pty Ltd (**WGA**) through a scheme of arrangement.

events.title: AtkinsRealis - WGA - Phase 1 Determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
AtkinsRéalis Group Inc. through its wholly owned subsidiary
AtkinsRéalis Australia Holdings Pty Ltd (AtkinsRéalis) proposes to
acquire 100% of the shares in WGA Group Holdings Pty Ltd (WGA)
(the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, AtkinsRéalis, is part of a publicly traded, Canadian
engineering and construction company, AtkinsRealis Group Inc. In
Australia, AtkinsRéalis operates as a professional engineering and
technical consultancy firm that provides engineering, design,
advisory and delivery services to public and private customers
across the transport, buildings and places, defence, energy and
water sectors.
The target, WGA, is a multi-disciplinary engineering and project
management consultancy firm, which operates across Australia and
New Zealand. It provides engineering, technical, advisory, delivery
and project management services as stand-alone commissions or
multi-disciplinary packages to both public and private clients across
the following sectors: buildings urban development, defence and
space, energy, ports and marine, resources, sports and recreation,
transport and water.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The parties overlap in the supply of engineering consultancy
services in Australia.
The Parties have on limited occasions entered into vertical
relationships (with WGA contracting AtkinsRéalis as a sub-
consultant or vice versa).

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties, and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• the Parties are not likely to be close competitors as they do not
commonly tender for the same projects: they operate at
materially different scales and have different geographic
presences, customer bases and sectoral focuses.
• The combined entity is expected to face competition from
alternative suppliers. The engineering consultancy services
industry is highly fragmented, with no single supplier holding a
material share of supply.
• the combined entity is unlikely to have the ability to foreclose
competitors, either upstream or downstream, due the availability
of alternative suppliers.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: WPF Duratec Pty Ltd (WPF Duratec), a wholly owned subsidiary of Australian engineering, construction and remediation contractor Duratec Limited (Duratec) (ASX:DUR), proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Davhold Australia Pty Ltd, trading as Pacific Welding Australia.

events.title: WPF Duratec / Pacific Welding Australia - Questionnaire
events.display_title: WPF Duratec / Pacific Welding Australia - Questionnaire

events.title: WPF Duratec - Pacific Welding - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
WPF Duratec Pty Ltd (WPF Duratec) proposes to acquire 100% of
the shares in Davhold Australia Pty Ltd, trading as Pacific Welding
Australia (Pacific Welding) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, WPF Duratec, is a wholly owned subsidiary of
Australian engineering, construction and remediation contractor
Duratec Limited (Duratec) (ASX:DUR). WPF Duratec is a specialist
engineering, fabrication and site services provider based in Western
Australia.
The target, Pacific Welding, is an Australian welding and fabrication
company based in Newcastle, New South Wales. Pacific Welding
specialises in the provision of project based welding, mechanical
services and specialist fabrication to clients in the oil & gas, energy
and mining industries.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
WPF Duratec and Pacific Welding (the Parties) overlap in the supply
of the following project-based welding, mechanical and labour hire
services (the Services):
• structural, mechanical and piping fabrication and installation
• pipelines and facilities construction
• vessel fabrication
• industrial asset maintenance services
• specialised welding services
• shutdown / outage / turnaround management.
There is limited geographic overlap between the Parties in the supply
of the Services as they operate in different states or territories (WPF
Duratec in Western Australia and the Northern Territory, and Pacific
Welding in New South Wales).
There is no existing vertical or conglomerate relationship between
the Parties, but there may be in the future absent the Acquisition as
subcontracting arrangements are common in the industry.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, in response to a request for information,
information from third parties and publicly available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings:
• There is limited geographic overlap between WPF Duratec and
Pacific Welding as they supply these services in different states
and territories. Therefore, the ACCC considers that the Parties
are not close competitors and likely compete for different
customers.
• The Parties would likely continue to be competitively constrained
by a range of alternative suppliers of the Services, including
AllType Engineering and Piping Solutions.
• The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give WPF
Duratec the ability or incentive to foreclose rivals accessing the
Services offered by Pacific Welding due to the existence of the
competitors identified above and the limited geographic overlap
between the Parties.
The ACCC received divergent views regarding competition from
other suppliers of the Services (beyond those identified above),
including Monadelphous, UGL, Civmec and M&S Fabrication, and the
extent to which these companies provide the full range of Services
offered by the Parties. The ACCC has taken this feedback into
account in its competition assessment.
The ACCC also received concerns from some market participants
regarding consolidation within the industry more generally. However,
these concerns were not sufficiently connected to a competition
issue arising from the Acquisition.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: GenusPlus Group Limited (**Genus**) proposes to acquire 100% of the issued shares in MPC Kinetic Holdings Pty Ltd (**MPK**) through a Share Purchase Agreement.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
GenusPlus Group Limited (Genus) applied for a notification waiver in
respect of its proposed acquisition of 100% of the issued share
capital in MPC Kinetic Holdings Pty Ltd (MPK) through a Share
Purchase Agreement, as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
9 June 2026

The acquirer, Genus, is a supplier of power and communications
infrastructure services. Through its infrastructure division, Genus
designs, builds and maintains high-voltage transmission and
distribution networks, substations, primary customer connections
and rail systems. Through its energy and engineering division, it
offers engineering design, and procurement and construction
solutions for electrical balance of plant (EBoP) services to
communications and energy assets, with a focus on electrical
systems such as Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) and grid
integration. Finally, through its services division, it constructs and
maintains fixed and wireless communication networks (including
nbn fibre and Telstra contracts), provides asset management and
upgrade services for power utilities, delivers vegetation management
for powerline corridors, and operates digital workforce management
platforms.
The target, MPK, provides specialised infrastructure and
maintenance services to the energy and resources sectors in
Australia and New Zealand. It installs gas gathering systems,
supplies civil balance of plant (CBoP) construction for onshore wind
farms, civil construction for BESS and mining civils. It also supplies
down-well maintenance and production supporting service lines -
workover emissions reduction technology, integrity monitoring, well
intervention services and well servicing.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
• a. there is no horizontal overlap in the supply of CBoP services
in Australia
b. there is limited horizontal overlap in the supply of EBoP
services in Australia
c. while there may be a non-horizontal relationship between the
products and services supplied by Genus and MPK, there are
alternative suppliers of these products and services in
Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: On 19 May 2026, Mimeca Nominees Pty Ltd as trustee for Mimeca Nominees Trust (**Mimeca Nominees**) entered into a Share Purchase Agreement to acquire 100% of the issued shares in Big Post Pty Ltd (**Big Post**)(the **Acquisition**). Mimeca Nominees and its connected entities trade under the name **Capital Group**.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Mimeca Nominees Pty Ltd as trustee for Mimeca Nominees Trust
(which alongside its connected entities, trade as Capital Group)
applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
acquisition of 100% of the issued shares in Big Post Pty Ltd (Big
Post) as described in the transaction documents provided as part of
the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
9 June 2026

The acquirer, Capital Group, is a transport and logistics service
provider. It supplies courier and freight services across metropolitan
(including Melbourne, Sydney, Brisbane, Perth and Adelaide), regional
and interstate Australia, to business and residential customers.
Capital Group’s operations are focussed on providing flexible access
to external carrier capacity for time-critical freight, with the majority
of its revenue derived from same-day metropolitan point-to-point
deliveries managed via a fleet of subcontractor owner-drivers.
Capital Group also supplies freight brokerage and logistics services
as part of its broader transport and logistics offering. This involves
warehousing services, and the arrangement and coordination of the
transport of goods for customers (including linehaul, home delivery,
courier, truck) by sourcing external carrier capacity from independent
third parties.
The target, Big Post, is a freight brokerage business operating from a
single office in Melbourne. It supplies road freight transport services
to customers located both within Melbourne and across other
Australian locations, with a particular focus on small-to-medium
enterprise customers across business-to-business, business-to-
consumer and business-to-depot channels.
Capital Group and Big Post both supply road freight services
(including freight brokerage services) in Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. the parties’ combined share in the supply of road freight
brokerage and road freight services to customers in
Australia is low, and the increase in share of supply arising
from the Acquisition would be small
b. there are alternative suppliers of road freight brokerage and
road freight services in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Mastercard Incorporated International (**Mastercard**) through its subsidiary Ares UK Holdco 2 Limited proposes to acquire BVNK Holdings Limited and B Corporation S.A.M (together, **BVNK**) from its current shareholders (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Mastercard - BVNK - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Philip Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Mastercard Incorporated International (Mastercard) through its
subsidiary Ares UK Holdco 2 Limited proposes to acquire BVNK
Holdings Limited and B Corporation S.A.M (together, BVNK) from its
current shareholders (the Acquisition)

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Mastercard is a technology company in the global
payments industry. Mastercard’s main activities include ownership
and operation of payment card schemes and provision of switching
services for card transactions. Mastercard provides a payments
network to facilitate the switching of payment transactions in fiat,
and offers payment capabilities that include automated clearing
house transactions as well as other payment-related services.
The target, BVNK supplies infrastructure services for stablecoin, a
form of cryptocurrency designed to keep a stable value, which is
typically achieved by being pegged to a traditional fiat currency such
as the US dollar. BVNK builds and supplies infrastructure to enable
customers to send, receive, convert, spend and earn stablecoins and
fiat currency across a variety of payment 'rails' and blockchains.
Neither Mastercard nor BVNK issues stablecoins.

The merger parties have a vertical relationship with BVNK's
stablecoin infrastructure services a potential input into the supply of
Mastercard's offerings to enable Mastercard to facilitate remittance
payments in stablecoin.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and publicly available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• Mastercard would not have the ability post-Acquisition to
foreclose rival stablecoin infrastructure service providers’
access fiat payment infrastructure providers as there are
multiple alternative fiat payments infrastructure service
providers available including Visa Inc, PayPal Australia, Stripe
Australia, American Express and Australian Payments Plus
(eftpos).
• Mastercard would also not have the ability post-Acquisition to
foreclose rival fiat payment infrastructure providers’ access to
stablecoin infrastructure services. BVNK has a limited presence
in Australia and there are multiple alternative stablecoin
infrastructure service providers available.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: FUJIFILM Australia Pty Ltd (**FUJIFILM Australia**) proposes to acquire all, or substantially all, of the FUJIFILM-branded endoscopy distribution business assets of C.R. Kennedy & Company Pty Ltd (**CRK**), including equipment, inventory (including spare parts), customer and supplier contracts, certain employees and the associated after sales service function in Australia (the **Acquisition**). The Acquisition will be implemented in two stages:

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
FUJIFILM Australia Pty Ltd (FUJIFILM Australia) applied for a
notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of all, or
substantially all, of the FUJIFILM-branded endoscopy distribution
business assets of C.R. Kennedy & Company Pty Ltd (CRK), including
equipment, inventory (including spare parts), customer and supplier
contracts, certain employees and the associated after sales service
function in Australia (the Acquisition).
The Acquisition will be implemented in two stages:
1. the transfer of the commercial rights of the FUJIFILM-
branded endoscopy distribution business will take effect on
1 July 2026; and
2. the transfer of the after sales services operations will follow
on 1 April 2027,
as described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
9 June 2026

The acquirer, FUJIFILM Australia, is part of the global FUJIFILM
group of companies, and is ultimately owned by FUJIFILM Holdings,
a listed company in Japan. FUJIFILM Australia operates across a
diverse range of industries, and supplies photographic imaging
solutions, commercial printing solutions, medical imaging solutions,
recording media and industrial products.
The endoscopy products are manufactured by FUJIFILM
Corporation, the group’s primary operating company in Japan.
FUJIFILM Corporation is a global manufacturer of endoscopy
equipment, namely endoscopes, processors and ancillary
equipment.
FUJIFILM Australia currently supplies its endoscopy products
exclusively through CRK. CRK is an importer and distributor of
photographic, hunting, geospatial, medical and surveillance
equipment. CRK's medical division supplies specialised medical
equipment, technology, software, and support services primarily to
the healthcare sector across Australia and New Zealand.
CRK supplies FUJIFILM-branded endoscopy products to hospitals,
day surgeries and medical clinics in Australia for use in endoscopic
procedures. CRK also provides servicing and preventative
maintenance for endoscopy equipment from its fully equipped
service repair centres in Melbourne and Sydney offering repairs on
equipment nationally using advanced diagnostic equipment, tools,
multi-function inspection and quality control tests.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular, there is no horizontal
consolidation or removal of a competitor as a result of the
Acquisition as it involves FUJIFILM Australia replacing CRK as the
exclusive distributor of FUJIFILM endoscopy products.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.

notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered

in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010

deals_cleared.merger_name: QIC – CopperString Project assets from Powerlink Queensland

acquirers.name: NORTH QUEENSLAND REGIONAL ENERGY CO PTY LTD

deals_cleared.merger_description: The Queensland Government has commissioned Queensland Investment Corporation (**QIC**) to oversee delivery of the CopperString Project, a proposed transmission line to be developed in North and Northwest Queensland, and to ensure the project is delivered efficiently while meeting the needs of those areas.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
In connection to the CopperString Project, subsidiaries of
Queensland Investment Corporation (QIC Limited) (QIC), QBF No. 2
Pty Ltd as trustee for the CopperString Project Assets Hold Trust
(SPV1 and SPV1 Trust respectively), and North Queensland Regional
Energy Co Pty Ltd as trustee for the North Queensland Regional
Energy Trust (SPV2 and SPV2 Trust respectively), applied for a
notification waiver in respect of the proposed transfer of certain
assets from Queensland Electricity Transmission Corporation
Limited (trading as Powerlink Queensland) (Powerlink), to the extent
it involves an acquisition of assets, any kind of property and/or legal
or equitable rights that are not property (the Acquisition).
The assets will be transferred to SPV1 (or another newly established
QIC subsidiary) acting in its capacity as trustee for SPV1 Trust, and
SPV2 as trustee for SPV2 Trust, pursuant to an asset transfer deed
and a regulation made under s 161 of the Government Owned
Corporations Act 1993 (QLD), as described in the documents
provided as part of the application. The assets being transferred
include contracts, land and physical assets (such as office
equipment).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
9 June 2026

The acquirers will both be subsidiaries of the Queensland Investment
Corporation, QIC. QIC is established under the Government Owned
Corporations Act 1993 (QLD) and Queensland Investment Corporation
Act 1991 (QLD). QIC is a leading global diversified alternative
investment manager with over AUD 131 billion in assets under
management (as at 30 June 2025). QIC manages interests in Tilt
Renewables and Pacific Energy, which provide electricity generation
services. QIC does not have interests in any electricity transmission
services in Australia.
The vendor, Powerlink, is also established under the GOC Act.
Powerlink develops, operates and maintains the high voltage
electricity transmission network in Queensland, supplying electricity
transmission network services and related transmission
infrastructure services. Powerlink’s network extends 1,700
kilometres from Cairns to the NSW border.
Both QIC and Powerlink are Queensland Government owned
corporations.
The CopperString Project is a major construction project to build
transmission lines and substations in North and Northwest
Queensland in two parts: the Eastern Link from Reid River (near
Townsville) to Hughenden and the Western Link from Hughenden to
Mount Isa.
The Queensland Government has commissioned QIC to oversee
delivery of the CopperString Project. SPV2 is ultimately intended to
be a vehicle which will allow for private sector investment in the
CopperString Project.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is little or no horizontal overlap between QIC and the
CopperString Project in the supply of transmission services
b. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
effects resulting from exclusionary conduct post-acquisition
c. the CopperString Project will be a regulated asset subject to
various non-discrimination obligations
d. the Queensland Government will continue to hold the
beneficial interest in the CopperString Project assets.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
The ACCC notes that it also considered whether the Acquisition fell
within the scope of sections 51ABU and 51ABV of the Act and the
potential application of section 51ABD (internal restructure and
reorganisations) of the Act. Having regard to the particular
circumstances, including the consequences of non-notification in
reliance on the exemption if it was found not to apply, the ACCC
considered it appropriate to make a determination on the basis that
the acquisition provisions in the Act apply to the Acquisition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Primary Wave KB Holdings, LP (together with its group companies, **Primary Wave**), intends to acquire substantially all of the interest units of Kobalt Music Group Topco L.P. (together with its subsidiaries, **Kobalt**) from Francisco Partners Management, L.P. (together with its affiliated funds, **Francisco Partners**) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Primary Wave-Kobalt - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Primary Wave KB Holdings, LP (together with its group companies,
Primary Wave), intends to acquire substantially all of the interest
units of Kobalt Music Group Topco L.P. (together with its
subsidiaries, Kobalt) from Francisco Partners Management, L.P. (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Primary Wave, is a US-based music acquisition,
publishing and talent management company engaged in acquiring,
managing and commercialising music rights and related intellectual
property of new songwriters and the catalogues of prominent artists
and songwriters.
The target, Kobalt, is a digital royalty collection and music publishing
administration company. Kobalt’s main business activities relate to
the provision of music publishing administration services to third-
party music rights holders. Kobalt’s music publishing services are
provided under the Kobalt brand for established artists and the
KOSIGN brand for emerging independent talent. Kobalt’s digital
royalty collection platform is provided under the “amra” brand.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The parties overlap in the supply of music publishing services.
Kobalt also supplies Primary Wave with music publishing and royalty
collection services.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and third parties, market share data from prior and
current other matters and publicly available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• Post-acquisition, the Parties’ market share aggregation is
estimated to be very low.
• The merged firm would continue to face competition from
alternative suppliers of music publishing services in Australia
and globally including Universal Music Australia, Sony Music
Entertainment, Warner Music Australia and BMG.
• Post-acquisition, Primary Wave is unlikely to have the ability or
incentive to foreclose rivals’ access to Kobalt’s music publishing
or royalty collection services due to the scale of its catalogue
and the presence of the alternative suppliers of music publishing
services, as noted above.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

partnerships include Barrenjoey, Vinva Investment Management and
FinClear Holdings.
The target, Barrenjoey, is an Australian-headquartered financial
services firm, primarily focused on offering mergers and acquisitions
advisory services, debt and equity capital markets services, equities
and fixed income trading and research services. Barrenjoey was
established in 2020 with initial funding and support provided by MFG
and Barclays PLC.

MFG and Barrenjoey overlap in relation to the supply of asset
management services in Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third partes, and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The Parties do not compete closely in the supply of asset
management services in Australia as they focus on different
asset classes and client types.
• The market share aggregation in the supply of asset
management services in Australia resulting from the Acquisition
is estimated to be low.
• The Parties would likely continue to face competition from
alternative suppliers of asset management services in Australia.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.
